text: The theme of wisdom acquisition is central to the unity of Sir 6:18-37. The whole section can be divided into three strands: initiation to the school of wisdom (6:18-22), obedience to wisdom (6:23-31), and the means to acquire wisdom (6:32-37). The focus of this study is on the first strand, that is, Sir 6:18-22. As mentioned here as two units of argumentation. The first unit, 6:18-19 is an agricultural pericope aimed at urging the disciple to persevere in his attempt to acquire wisdom; the instruction in the second unit, 6:20-22 is addressed to the uneducated, heartless person, who because of his lack of patience and lack of will to receive an education, is unlikely to be wise. In between, we have the wisdom that is within the reach of everyone, the reason why only few are wise. Wisdom is acquired only by those who are willing to endure the period of “schooling” or “education” that is necessary for life (6:18). Even though those who will benefit from the fruits of wisdom education will soon gain their reward, one must indeed endure long training to acquire wisdom (6:18). The uneducated and heartless man, in fact, is one who is not prepared to endure the period of initiation and therefore has no chance to be wise (6:20). Since the uneducated and heartless man is not able to endue the period of schooling for wisdom, he will not have wisdom. As we have already seen wisdom is possible for all (cf. Sir 6:22). For it is not an external acquisition that prevents others from having wisdom, but an internal one, that of the disposition and qualities of mind necessary to acquire wisdom, that is only present in few.
headline: Fully Human Written
